text: I'd started martial arts before the Bruce Lee era came in.
I told you, Bruce Lee came in in around 77, I think.
And oh my God, at that time we were like heroes, you know.
We knew some martial art.
He was a genius.
He was a genius. His philosophy, his fighting style.
If there is the last word in self-defense, it is Bruce Lee.
But it was so heartbreaking not to go to that Olympics.
Later on, to see that your name is in the Olympic draw.
So just a while ago, I came to know
that I am a double Olympian.
If you Google my name, I've gone to two Olympics.
But 88, I was sitting at home and watching the bloody opening ceremony with tears in my eyes.
And then subsequently, I meet Rajiv Gandhi at the Parsi Dharamshala opening.
He says, how was the experience?
I said, no, I didn't go.
That's it.
Episode number 250 is an emotional episode for me.
Lately, we've been doing a lot of subject matter based podcasts on the Ranveer show.
Not done one of those open, heartfelt episodes in a while.
Today's guest is my first guru in life.
He's my sensei.
He's my judo coach.
He's been a father figure to me.
He's been the first mentor I've ever had since the age of 10.
Since the age that I began practicing and diving deeper into the world of judo and martial arts.
Kawas Bilimouria Sir is an Arjuna awardee.
He's a former Olympian.
He's represented India in sumo wrestling as well.
He's lived in Japan, honing his martial arts skills, learning the philosophies related to
martial arts and bringing it back to India.
I'm going to say something about myself in this introduction, which is that it's been
a difficult seven year journey.
That's the truth about content creation and business.
It becomes even more difficult when you try combining both and growing your career along
with growing lots of other people's careers with you.
It's been challenging.
And at every moment that I felt a sense of challenge, I've gone back to the 10 year old
version of myself trying to imbibe the martial arts mentalities from this human being.
Very special episode for me.
There's a part of me that is not bothered about how this one performs because I had to do
this one was in my heart.
You'll feel it if you listen to this entire episode.
This is Kawas Bilimouria Sir, my Sensei on TRS.
For more episodes like this, make sure you follow us on Spotify.
Spotify exclusive, which means that every episode is available on Spotify 48 hours before
it's available anywhere else in the world.
Before I let you slip into this one, deep thank you from my heart for taking us to 250
English episodes.
There's about 120 or 130 Hindi episodes that we've released as well.
I don't keep a track of the numbers unless it's a milestone episode like this one.
250 had to be with Kawas Sir.
This is my Sensei presented for you and I hope that you're able to gain some of those
fighter mentality, some of those martial arts mentalities that he transferred to my mind
and heart between the ages of 10 and 16.
Lots of love to you, sir.
And lots of love to the audience.
Enjoy the episode.
Episode number 250, absolute dream episode.
I remember episode 50 was with my mother.
And after that episode was received well, I really thought of who I wanted to bring on.
And the honest truth is before I would bring on my own dad, I would bring on my own
Sensei.
And Sensei, for those people who don't understand the word, is basically your martial
arts coach.
Martial arts is never just about the moves, the physical tactics.
It's so much about the mind.
It's so much about spiritual growth in so many ways.
This is my first mentor in life.
Like right when you turn 10, when you're a preteen, you need mentorship, according to
me. And I needed it for so many reasons.
Cowers Bill Moria, sir, and Arjuna Awaddy and my first mentor in life.
I feel so much of everything that's happened in my life has happened because you taught
me the right lessons when I was 10 years old, 11 years old, 15 years old.
So firstly, thank you, sir.
Thank you.
Thank you for that, Ranveer.
Really, you've been a model student.
Let me tell everybody.
I mean, Ranveer, you've been a model student.
You worked hard.
That was number one.
Winning, losing so many medals at MSSA was not what was important.
You just trained to the best of your ability every time you were on that judo mat.
And that made you special.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I have so much to tell you.
There's things I'm going to tell you on this podcast that I've not told my own parents
that I've wanted to tell you for a very long time.
But, you know, over the course of your career, my career is creative in nature.
So in a creative career, you do so much self-exploration that you get to know aspects of yourself.
So as I've inched towards age 30, I've realized so much about you and the impact that you
specifically had on my life.
I went for one of these self-exploration forums and they asked me to name the three most
amount of gurus I had in my life.
The first one I could name very easily, which was you.
Thank you so much.
Yeah.
I couldn't think of really two others to kind of fill those spots, but you were there.
So, I mean, how many students have you even trained now in like martial arts?
Because you've had an impact on so many people.
I'll tell you, I started teaching in January, 1986.
I founded my own club, the Ippon Judo Academy.
And I returned from Japan in 1985.
After six months, I planned a three-month stay.
At the end of the second month, I broke my fifth metatarsal.
That is the little bone, you know.
Now imagine I had a visa for three months and I had enough money for three months in
Tokyo, but how could I have come back broken and injured?
No way.
Because I had always imagined that when I come back from there, I should be better
than the best and better than what I ever was.
And that was not the condition I wanted to come back.
So what did I do?
You won't believe it.
I had no money to go to a hospital.
And one of the coaches there, the old coaches, Judo coaches in Japan, all knew
bone setting and first aid.
So he told me, your bone has not really shifted out of place.
There is a definite break because he could feel it and feel it in the sense, the pain
I was going through and the immediate swelling.
So he said, it's the fifth metatarsal.
And he said, I'll just do a tight stick wrap, stick, stick, stick, stick on, and it
should heal within three, three weeks.
I said, great.
He did it.
After the next day, the next day, I went hobbling for at least a kilometer and a half
to a place where wholesale sporting goods are sold.
It's called Okachimachi or whatever in Tokyo.
And there under the train railway tracks, I find these size 17 basketball shoes.
My size is 15 US.
So I find size 17.
For those people who can't tell, so it's like about six, five.
How tall are you?
Six, five.
Six, four and a half and shrinking now.
I called my friend and I said, you know, you had a job in mind for me.
He said, yeah, anytime go and serve at the restaurant.
Second day after my broken foot, I was at this restaurant, the Maharaja in Tokyo.
It still exists in Ginza.
I didn't know what I was getting into.
So I was called at eight o'clock.
I hobbled there.
I didn't tell anyone my foot was broken.
They said, your job is first thing, clean the tables, lay the tables, clean the brass
and the glass with newspaper and spray.
After that, go up, wear the Maharaja outfit and the turban specially.
Go outside and give the discount coupons for lunch and then wait for lunch to start.
And then only two things.
Clear the tables, three things.
Clear the table, lay the tables, serve water.
It was so hard.
Under normal circumstances, being national champion, I would have found it hard
because all the tables are low in Japan.
So I had to bend so much more.
I was hobbling around and around that restaurant and I don't know how the five days,
that week got over.
I just don't know how it got over.
But the good thing is I got free breakfast, free lunch.
They even offered that you come and have dinner free.
But I wasn't too much pain to walk back at night.
So I had the free lunch, the free dinner and I got a salary of 90 rupees
or whatever the yen equivalent was per hour.
And that made me so strong because I got super breakfast.
There were tandoors like this, you know, where authentic tandoori was made.
And it was heaven after that because Japan is so expensive that I was always
running short of food and my foot broke because maybe not enough nutrition.
But after that, there was no looking back.
I continued working.
I extended my visa for another three months and I was getting money.
And I was training early in the morning before going to the restaurant,
finishing my restaurant, sleeping for an hour after my training and then training
for four hours in the evening.
And it was so good.
I came back from there and I didn't lose a national championship for the next nine years.
Wow.
So, you know, you have to give something to get something.
Yeah.
You've been a national champion for the longest time.
I remember these stories even in school, sir.
What does it take to actually become a national champion of a sport?
I'll tell you.
That something in the Mahabharata, something said,
just do things without expecting anything.
You know what I mean?
Yeah.
Just strive.
And if you strive sensibly and hard, both.
Yeah.
Things will happen.
And I guess they happened.
Everything happens.
Sometimes, my nature is not so pushy.
I don't push for things.
Maybe that's a drawback which I have.
But everything comes a little late though.
So I've won so many national titles.
I won a Commonwealth silver.
I won an Asian bronze.
I went to the Olympics.
And yet the Arjuna Award came only in 1994.
Up till the age of 10, I had not even really played any sport.
I was still finding my sport.
And I wasn't really good at football or cricket or anything.
And my parents told me, wait till you turn 10, then we'll put you in martial arts.
Because even when I was a kid, I was drawn towards martial arts.
I didn't know.
I watched a lot of Bruce Lee films.
All that.
And I knew that I had to be there.
So, fifth grade when I joined school, it's actually when school started in 2003.
I saw this 6'5 man walking around looking super athletic.
I was like, who's that?
And I was naturally drawn to you.
Then I found out that you're the judo coach and two of my wishes kind of met.
So I decided, okay, I'm going to take judo classes in school.
And like right from the first time you taught that hip throw, oh, Goshi.
I remember there was like eight other students with me.
And you said, okay, who wants to be thrown?
You threw your deputy that time.
I think it wasn't Khushnoor sir, it was someone else.
You threw your deputy that time.
And there was this loud sound.
Everyone got scared.
But I really wanted to be thrown.
Oh my God.
I put up my arm and I said, okay, sir, just throw me.
And you did a judo takedown on me.
Like the most basic judo takedown.
And I just fell in love.
I wanted to master the sport after that.
I thought it was just about technique and moves and strength.
And there was so much more.
I asked my mom that, you know, in her eyes, what was the turning point in my life?
And even she takes your name.
She says, judo.
When you began judo, things started coming together.
Now, apparently this is something I've never told you before.
Around the same time, I started having very intense family problems at home.
Like a lot of things were happening and I didn't, I was too, I wasn't mature enough to
understand what was happening at home.
So there was a lot of aggression building up inside me.
If I didn't have judo as an outlet, I don't know what would have happened to my mind.
Um, so it saved me.
It protected me in like so many ways, which I've understood as a man in his late 20s,
which is why I've kind of wanted to go back to it as well.
But my question to you is as a sensei, are you conscious of this non-physical
aspect of martial arts?
Like how it's changing kids?
Let's talk about self-esteem.
I am pretty sure that when one starts mastering the basic throw or technique, something
he's adding more than just the throw.
He's getting stronger.
He starts looking better.
His or her self-image improves and therefore slowly it translates to his self-esteem.
Unfortunately, parents today come and tell me my child has been doing judo for the past
three to six months as a core activity and my child hasn't won a single medal.
What will happen to his or her self-esteem in the first place?
Why should that child be having an esteem in judo?
I mean, I mean, why don't you just learn it for the sake of learning it?
What makes you feel that you deserve a medal?
As I told you, you told me what, why did I do judo?
I just did it.
I don't know why my self-esteem was improving and my mom was paying the fees and dad.
And we were three brothers.
We ate a lot in the house and money was hard.
Okay.
Let me tell you.
Mom was giving tuitions and even 30 rupees a month was hard.
And the most important, my self-esteem was getting stronger.
And I was developing.
I was losing.
I told you it didn't matter.
It didn't matter.
I was last in the class in every reading.
I was not good at memorizing the names of techniques in Japanese.
Could not memorize so well, nor was I good at performing the throw.
I was bulky, fat all over the place and it was a disaster.
Okay.
So why did I continue?
My self-esteem was improving.
Now, what does the parent come and do?
My child's self-esteem is not improving.
Who are you to decide that your child's self-esteem is not improving?
But they decide.
And in the bargain, they may pull the child out.
It doesn't matter when the child will win.
This particular child could be me.
The child could start winning after six years.
After seven years.
Boom!
One day.
Boom!
Do you feel martial arts as a culture, like popularity is increasing in the world
because of MMA and UFC and things like that?
Absolutely.
But the times which I saw during the Bruce Lee era, you know,
I had started martial arts before the Bruce Lee era came in.
I told you, Bruce Lee came in in around 77, I think.
And oh my God, at that time, we were like heroes, you know.
We knew some martial arts.
And when Bruce Lee did a shoulder throw in any of his movies,
oh, we celebrated it.
Yeah, truly, he was a genius.
He was a genius.
His philosophy, his fighting style and yeah.
If there is the last word in self-defense,
it is Bruce Lee and his art of Jeet Kundo.
The art of intercepting the fist.
Can you explain the philosophy of it?
Other martial arts compartmentalized fighting.
In the sense you blocked and you punched, right?
Or you blocked and you punched, right?
Or you blocked and you punched.
Or you parried and you punched and you parried and you punched.
Bruce Lee blocked and attacked with the same hand.
And if he attacked, it always meant business, right?
If there was a serious someone with you with a knife,
it was nothing but gouging the eyes.
Straight.
No, no, no nonsense about it.
Or breaking the knee or kicking the groin.
Yeah, there was then no hanky panky that will punch you here and we'll try and no.
It was very practical and straight on.
So a block attack would be the same.
Your left hand would be for the opponent's right.
Very compartmentalized in that way.
My right hand would be there and my legs would block his kicks.
Whatever kicks he gave, I would block with my leg.
Genius.
Absolutely phenomenal.
Other martial arts would say kick with the back foot.
Bruce Lee will say just break the knee or just break the groin.
You don't need much power.
Otherwise, when you kick from the back foot, honestly, you're telegraphing.
Hello, I'm coming.
And it's not going to work on the street, right?
Yeah.
So, he's phenomenal.
You don't need so much power to break someone's nose.
Yeah.
Six inches is enough for me.
Just six inches.
And that's enough.
It'll smash the nose.
What's this black belt journey like?
Like from the moment you get it and beyond getting your first black belt,
when you go up the first dan, second dan, third dan.
What's that journey like?
So, it depends on the different martial arts, right?
In Judo, you are a fighting black belt till you're the fourth dan in Japan.
That means I am perhaps the only fighting third dan from India, right?
I have won the red and white tournament to get my belt.
Okay.
I won the tournament.
I demonstrated my kata in Japan and I got my third dan from Kodokan.
Many people have got their third dan from Kodokan in India, perhaps even more.
But I know that they have not fought and won all their points in the shiai.
I know that in the Kohaku shiai, red and white shiai.
I was fortunate that I had the time to spend there.
I made the time.
I begged and borrowed for funds, for sponsors.
I begged.
There were some people who would tell you,
yes, they would sponsor you completely and the next day not take your call.
There were some people who insulted you and put you down,
but the next day they gave you the money.
And there were some people who said,
it fits into my constitution, in my trust constitution.
Here's your check.
Right?
So that itself was a big learning.
That everything you see and everything you hear may not come true.
The very next day, the person who has promised you the full sponsorship may not pick up your phone.
So I took 3000 rupees, 2000 rupees and went to Japan.
Anyway, then the second time I went, I was an employee of Tata Steel.
And that made it relatively easier.
But look at the irony.
We were allowed to take only 500 dollars.
Only 500 dollars when you left the country.
Now, where will 500 dollars take me in Japan?
It would not even have taken me half a month.
Right?
So to go in 1989 to Japan, I had to meet V.P. Singh.
And thanks to one Mr. Dossu Gandhi, I met Mr. V.P. Singh.
And on the way to the airport, he sanctioned whatever I needed for the trip.
2000 dollars.
So it was a struggle.
The journey of the black belt in our time.
Today, kids in school are getting the black belt.
So many children of our school have got the black belt.
And they deserve it.
I got my black belt after becoming national champion.
So I started judo in, as I said, 1972-73.
And I got my black belt in 1984.
They presented it to me because I became open weight champion of India.
So the journey is hard.
Second and third down hard because I got it from Tokyo.
I used to go win four fights in a row on Monday.
Then the next week, another two.
What are the tests?
Like what are the examinations?
You have to, every week, there is a Shia contest.
You have to win your points in the Shia.
If you win, you stay.
So I won, won, won!
Three!
One, one, one, four.
Okay, I've qualified.
Then I have to wait out the time period and give my Kata, the form.
Okay, second down.
Then same thing for third down.
What I also find cool is that you've done sumo wrestling.
I want to know why.
And did you actually have to bulk up?
That was hilarious.
And honestly, those were fun times.
In 1995, I have a fifth place in the world championship.
Not because maybe I was a great sumo fighter,
but maybe others were not so good, other countries.
And yeah, honestly, we were sailing in the same boat
because sumo became an amateur sport and I was the early worm.
So the secretary of the All Japan Sumo Federation caught me after the Kano Cup.
He saw me there fighting versus the world champion, Ogawa.
Straight from there, I went into the Sumo Dohyo.
You can Google it.
The Nichidai Sumo Club.
One of the most popular sumo clubs.
One station outside Shinjuku.
I think Nakano is the name of the station.
Just out of Tokyo.
Beautiful Dohyo.
Beautiful training center.
And I lived there for one month or two months initially for training.
You have to eat Junko Nabe and all that.
Junko Nabe. Oh my God.
Oh my God.
I mean, explain it to the listeners.
So Junko is a dish where there's a broth.
The pot is boiling and we start training at three o'clock
and the pot starts boiling and the rice starts cooking.
And in the broth, you put all sorts of...
One day you would have all the meats in the world.
Whatever the meat you want, you chuck in.
One day it would be all fish.
And there would be alcohol, beer.
Beer, beer, beer.
Really?
And it would be predominantly in the Junko.
Yeah, it would cook and sometimes wine.
And then every fried food possible from chicken to pork.
You know, tender pork, cuts.
I have never eaten such good quality and quantity food anywhere.
In Judo, it was always next to starvation.
And over here, it was heaven.
They would say, everything was on the house.
I would be a guest.
Ranveer, for six years, seven years, they flew me.
What seven?
From 1990 to 2002, they flew me to Tokyo and back.
I was like Bruce Lee going to Hans Island to fight.
It was amazing. Amazing.
But the journey.
Do they actually try to fatten you up?
Because it's an advantage.
They do.
There is a weight category under 115 kilos.
So I chose that.
Because it would have been very hard to get back.
Okay.
And other guys like as tall as you?
Yeah, I've played 300 kilos and brought the boy down to India also.
Really?
Yeah.
You've played someone who's 300 kilos.
300 kilos.
Emmanuel Yabro, the heaviest athlete in the world.
What was it like facing him?
I mean, he just did boom.
And I went back and back and back and back and back.
Yeah.
Emmanuel was a great guy.
He was once the world champion in amateur sumo.
Open weight, beating the Japanese and the Mongols.
So the only people I've lost to in sumo.
Yeah, actually three countries.
I lost my world championship third place to a Frenchman.
I lost once to a Mongolian.
And I lost to a Japanese the other two times for my gold medal.
What's it like entering a ring with a 300 kilo person but mentally?
No, no, no.
I'll tell you what.
I enjoyed sumo.
There was no stress.
I enjoyed it.
What are the rules?
There's a ring and you're supposed to push the other guy out of the ring.
Yeah, either you push him or make any other part of his body touch the
ground before you fall.
Okay.
So twice for my silver medal in the Asian championships, I used to lift my opponent
on one side, right with the belt with his.
And then I would bring him on his toes and then switch.
So after bringing me, he's trying to get his weight back there and then I would
switch both the times I fell, but his hand, his hand was not touching the ground.
His right hand would touch before I fell.
So strangely in both the cases in the final for my pool final to go into the
final in both the cases, the center referee, Shimpan gave the decision
against me, but he was overruled by the five judges sitting around.
And both the times I reached the final like that.
Once I beat Kazakhstan and once I beat someone else.
Yeah, Ranbir.
So sumo was amazing.
It was a dream.
I, I, I can't believe that I've gone through these things in life today.
When I look back, I fought in the Ryogoku, Kokugui Kakan.
Ryogoku is the ultimate place for sumo fighters in the heart of Tokyo.
Does it take a toll on your body?
Like training for sumo?
Honestly, you know, I never got an injury through sumo.
Okay.
And I've fallen off the dojo with a hundred and fifteen kilo Japanese on me.
And from this height on clay, but strangely nothing happens.
Maybe it's that salt we throw before we fight.
No, no, that's to drive away the evil spirits.
Wow.
Do you, do you remember me as a student?
Obviously.
What, what do you remember?
Diligent, absolutely disciplined, never missed a session, hard work,
cool as a cucumber before the fights and mostly coming out victorious.
How many, how many medals did you win in the MSSA?
Mumbai School Sports Association.
From eighth standard onwards, everything.
So three years back to back goals.
And I think I won a few DSOs also here and there.
That's really awesome.
You know, it's not easy.
MSSA has so many competitors.
Yeah.
It, it, it was a very important character building phase in my teenage for sure.
But I remember this one tournament that I've spoken about on the show in sixth grade,
where out of four guys, I finished fourth and it was my first competitive tournament.
And you didn't, you didn't really reprimand me or anything.
You said, okay, it's fine.
We'll, we'll pick up and we'll go from here.
So I sat in my car, I went home with my mom and I was like crying because I didn't like losing,
especially after that level of dedication that put in from fifth grade and all of sixth grade.
And even she told me that, no, you've probably not just not won because you've not put in
the hard work required.
The number of hours.
Yeah.
The number of hours.
Yeah, somewhere it comes to that rule of number of hours, but sensible hours.
Yeah.
That's true for sport, right?
Like the more you train, the more the nervous system gets worked.
Absolutely.
And even when I'm sitting and I'm focusing on my favorite technique,
like I explained to you that technique, which I did, believe you me, all the muscles are working.
When I'm doing the technique that all, even my legs are working,
but not enough to generate movement, but mentally everything is working.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's just amazing.
So that visualization, the deep breathing, the meditation, the mental preparation.
I didn't know all that, man.
I just fought.
It's okay.
The only time I took a sports psychologist's help, I lost.
That was the only time I lost in the Indian nationals after 10 years in Pune.
I started my career, national career in Pune, where I got strangled on consciousness in 81
and it ended in 94, where I broke the shoulder.
So I don't have a front deltoid.
You don't know how hard it is.
You qualify for an Olympics, like in 1988 with the bronze medal.
I qualified in 86.
I qualified for the 88 Olympics.
And my name and Mr. Sandeep Biala, who was fighting 65 kilos bronze medal winner again,
our names were sent up to the international judo federation.
And I was going to Mr. Rajiv Gandhi's office, the prime minister's office, okay.
Every alternate night to tell his PA, forget the names now, to help me.
Along with the then sports minister or minister of youth affairs or whatever,
they sent an eight member relay team and through Sandeep Biala and me out.
They didn't send us to the Olympics.
Later on, I came to know that they are the ones who didn't send me.
And I used to go and appeal to them to send me.
It's weird.
And I know you go back, Google it.
The relay team, India relay team of 2000 and of 1988.
And you'll read the fiasco.
Those girls refused to do a trial in India.
Can you imagine?
All eight went to Seoul at the Olympic stadium and had a trial four days before as to who will run.
And obviously they came last in the first eight.
A champion athlete, no names I'm mentioning, was feigning injury and she was injured.
All of them were injured.
They came nowhere.
And at whose cost?
My cost and Sandeep's cost.
I'm clear about that today.
I'm absolutely clear.
It was hard not going to the Olympics.
When you are at your peak, man, you're at your peak.
You believe you can win.
You believe.
I believe that other than Japan, France, Russia, Korea, I'll beat everyone else.
I believed when I went in 92.
That belief was not.
When I went in 1992, I went with the thing that I will do my best.
No, you can't go like that.
You have to go to win.
I can't fool myself.
Right?
It took me how many nationals to win a gold?
Three nationals.
I'm not some brilliant guy.
I'm not a guy who can just excel from word.
It takes me time.
Maybe second Olympics, I would have been better prepared.
Maybe.
But it was so heartbreaking not to go to that Olympics.
And later on to see that your name is in the Olympic draw.
So just a while ago, I came to know that I'm a double Olympian.
If you Google my name, I've gone to two Olympics, 88 and 92.
But 88, I was sitting at home and watching the bloody opening ceremony with tears in my eyes.
And go to whom?
To which press?
Complain to whom?
My whole career would have been destroyed by them.
And then subsequently, I meet Rajiv Gandhi at the Parsi Dharamshala opening.
He says, how was the experience?
I said, no, I didn't go.
That's it.
But I guess that's the nature of sport.
And it teaches you the nature of life as well.
Which is why in this particular episode, sir, I have one last question for my Sensei.
At 30, what advice would you give me?
I'm going to be turning 30 in about 7-8 months from now.
What do you have to say?
Because you've known me since I was a 9-year-old.
